key_insight: A reduced-basis hybrid TBNN-UDE framework can learn frame-invariant viscoelastic constitutive laws from shear data and still generalize to more complex flow conditions.
engineering_relevance: This enables more data-efficient digital twins for computational rheology, with constitutive models that can be embedded directly in CFD solvers for predicting viscoelastic flow behavior.
method: Train hybrid Tensor Basis Neural Network + Universal Differential Equation models on synthetic shear-flow data.
Use a reduced tensor-basis formulation to improve physical consistency and numerical stability.
Embed the learned constitutive law into an open-source finite volume solver for runtime evaluation in CFD.
evidence_signal: The models were trained only on oscillatory shear datasets generated from established viscoelastic laws and then evaluated by extrapolation to extensional flows and 2D/3D benchmarks such as sudden contraction and cross-slot cases.
watch_item: Performance degrades as the test flows move farther from the training regime, so extrapolation to strong 3D and high-Deborah-number conditions remains a key risk.
paper_title: Harnessing Machine Learning for Hybrid Constitutive Modelling of Viscoelastic Fluid Flows in Computational Rheology
